title: Fitting a bottle trap to a basin waste
subtitle: Replace an old trap or fit a new chrome bottle trap to a bathroom basin
excerpt: How to fit a chrome or plastic bottle trap to a bathroom basin waste outlet. Covers removing the old trap, connecting the waste to the tail of the trap, and adjusting the discharge direction to connect to the waste pipe.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 6465-1: Sanitary installations. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989). Plumbing trade practice; general (UK).

glossaryTerms.definition: A compact trap for a basin waste, shaped like a bottle standing upright. Water enters from the top (the basin waste tail), fills the lower section of the bottle, and exits through a horizontal outlet pipe at the bottle's lower section. The water retained in the bottle provides the water seal (minimum 75 mm depth) that prevents sewer gases entering the room. More visually compact than a P-trap or S-trap but harder to clean if a blockage forms.

The depth of water held in a trap that physically blocks the passage of sewer gases back into the room. Building Regulations require a minimum seal depth of 75 mm for a basin trap. If the seal is siphoned out (by a fast-emptying bath or the wrong pipe configuration), the trap is effectively absent and the room will smell. Bottle traps with a deep-seal version (75 mm) resist siphonage better than a standard 38 mm seal trap.

content.text: is the standard trap for a pedestal basin where the waste is visible; its compact chrome or white finish is neater than a bend trap in an exposed position. Replacing a trap is a 15-minute job: the trap unscrews from the basin waste tail and the waste pipe, and the new one fits the same way. The only dimension that matters is the connection size: standard basins use 32 mm or 40 mm waste. Check before purchasing the replacement.

A bottle trap in the correct connection size (32 mm for most basins; check by measuring the existing waste tail or waste pipe). An adjustable spanner. A bucket or bowl to catch residual water. Plumber's PTFE tape. A damp cloth.

Put the bucket under the trap to catch the water it holds. Unscrew the large slip nut at the top of the trap (where it connects to the basin waste tail); turn anticlockwise by hand or with the adjustable spanner. Unscrew the slip nut at the outlet end where the trap connects to the waste pipe. Pull the old trap away. Check the condition of the basin waste tail; if the chrome is deteriorated or the rubber seal is hard, replace the waste fitting itself at this point.

The bottle trap consists of: a top inlet section with a slip nut and washer; the bottle body; and a horizontal outlet section that can rotate to align with the waste pipe. Slide the slip nut and washer onto the basin waste tail. Push the top section of the trap up to the tail, slide the slip nut down, and tighten hand-tight. Rotate the outlet section of the trap to align with the waste pipe. Push the outlet into the waste pipe (or connect with the slip nut if both ends are threaded). Tighten both slip nuts hand-tight plus a quarter-turn with the spanner.
Step 3, check the water seal and test
Run the basin for 30 seconds and check all connections for drips. With the basin full, let the plug out and watch: the trap should hold a

after the basin empties (you can unscrew the bottom of the bottle body to check: water should be visible in the base section). If the seal is absent (if the bottom empties completely) the waste pipe configuration may be siphoning the trap. Fit a deep-seal bottle trap (75 mm seal) or an anti-siphon valve to the waste pipe.

attrs.symptom: Slow drain after fitting the new trap
attrs.cause: Trap outlet not fully aligned with the waste pipe, creating a partial obstruction.
attrs.fix: Loosen the outlet slip nut, adjust the rotation of the outlet to align more directly with the waste pipe, and retighten.

attrs.symptom: Drip at the top connection between the trap and the basin waste tail
attrs.cause: Washer seated incorrectly or missing; slip nut not tight enough.
attrs.fix: Unscrew the slip nut, remove and reseat the rubber washer squarely, and retighten. Overtightening the chrome nut can crack the fitting — hand-tight plus a quarter turn is sufficient.
